abstract: Background: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) is a leading cause of morbidity and mortality. Objective: To compare the efficacy of Drug A versus Drug B in reducing exacerbation rates in patients with moderate to severe COPD. Methods: In this multicenter, double-blind, randomized controlled trial, 512 patients were assigned in a 1:1 ratio to receive either Drug A or Drug B once daily for 12 months. The primary endpoint was the annual rate of moderate-to-severe exacerbations. Secondary outcomes included lung function and quality of life. Results: The exacerbation rate was 1.1 per year in the Drug A group and 1.4 in the Drug B group (rate ratio 0.79; 95% CI, 0.64–0.98; P=0.03). Improvements in FEV1 and St George's Respiratory Questionnaire scores favored Drug A. Safety profiles were comparable between the groups. Conclusion: Drug A was more effective than Drug B in reducing COPD exacerbations, with a similar safety profile. These findings support the use of Drug A as a preferred option in this population.
sample_size_bias: No power analysis reported (n=512): Unable to verify adequate study power for effect detection
primary_outcome_events_bias: Primary outcome results not clearly reported: unable to assess event rates
